title: Shri Pakkhi Khamana (Fortnightly Asking-of-Forgiveness)
summary: The Pakkhi Khamana is a short devotional song of forgiveness sung near the close of the fortnightly (Pakkhi) pratikramana. In it the worshipper turns, one by one, to every member of the spiritual community — the Arihants and liberated Siddhas, the Acharyas, Upadhyayas and all monks, and the lay men and women — and asks their pardon for any offence, naming each by the count of their good qualities (guna). It ends by appeasing the whole Sangha and bowing in forgiveness to the two great teachers of this gaccha, Aryarakshitasuri and Kalyanasagarasuri, and to the present head of the order. It is sung because pratikramana is incomplete without khamana — the mutual release of resentment that restores peace within the community.
ritualPlacement: Within this book the Pakkhi Khamana stands near the end of the Pakkhi (fortnightly) pratikramana, after the Nine Smaranas and before the closing colophon. Because the book gives the Chaumasi (four-monthly) and Samvatsari (annual) pratikramanas no independent text — they are performed exactly as the Pakkhi but with the word 'pakkhi' replaced by 'chaumasi' or 'samvatsari' (and the kayotsarga lengthened) — this same khamana is recited in all three. Indeed verse 4 names its own occasions: atham, pakkhi, chaumasi (thrice yearly) and samvatsari. It is a vernacular khamana-geet (forgiveness-song), distinct from the formal khamasamana bows, and belongs to the spirit of the fourth and sixth Avashyakas (reflection/atonement and renewal) rather than being one of the six Avashyakas itself.
authorAndSource: Author not named in the source book; the composition is a classical-Gujarati devotional geet in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) tradition, transmitted within 'Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit'. Its closing verses fix it firmly to this gaccha by invoking Dada Shri Aryarakshitasuri — counted the 47th patta from Ganadhara Sudharmasvami and codifier of the gaccha's shuddha vidhi — as 'ornament of the Vidhipaksha-gaccha', and Kalyanasagarasuri as 'ornament of the Anchalgaccha', followed by the reigning gacchadhipati of the day. Specific authorship and date are not attested in the book and are left uncertain.
structureArc: The seven verses move down the ladder of reverence and then outward to occasion and lineage. v1: ask forgiveness of the Arihants (12 gunas) and Siddhas (8 gunas). v2: of the Acharyas (36 gunas) and Upadhyayas (25 gunas). v3: of all Sadhus (27 gunas) and of the Shravakas and Shravikas (21 gunas) — together these guna-counts trace the 108 qualities of the five Parameshthins plus the layfolk's virtues. v4: name the occasions of khamana — atham, pakkhi, chaumasi (three times a year), samvatsari — 'again and again'. v5: appease the displeased Sangha repeatedly, then bow in forgiveness to Aryarakshitasuri and Kalyanasagarasuri. v6: extend the same forgiveness to the present gacchadhipati. v7: close with the Chaumasi guru who expounds the sutra and siddhanta.
recurringImagery: The whole song is built on one repeated devotional gesture, carried by its refrain 'khamo bhavi khamana re' — 'O fortunate soul, forgive!' Each verse pairs a class of beings with the number of their distinguishing qualities (guna): Arihant 12, Siddha 8, Acharya 36, Upadhyaya 25, Sadhu 27, Shravaka/Shravika 21. This guna-counting is not decoration but a litany of recognition — to ask someone's forgiveness one first acknowledges their worth — and the first five counts deliberately sum to the 108 gunas of the Pancha-Parameshthin praised in the Navkar. A second recurring motif is the word 'shanagar' (ornament/adornment): each great teacher is hailed as the 'ornament' of his gaccha, so that lineage-devotion and forgiveness become a single act. Both motifs are explained here once; the per-verse notes need only point to them.
keyThemes: kshamapana — mutual asking and granting of forgiveness
reverence to the five Parameshthins and the four-fold Sangha
the occasions of khamana: atham, pakkhi, chaumasi, samvatsari

glossary.definition: Asking-of-forgiveness; the act (and the song) of seeking pardon and releasing resentment.
The Sanskrit term for mutual forgiveness, the spiritual heart of pratikramana.
A bhavya soul — one capable of liberation; here a vocative, 'O fortunate one'.
A virtue or distinguishing quality; each rank of being is named by the fixed count of its gunas.
A Jina who has conquered the inner enemies; honoured with 12 gunas.
A fully liberated, embodiment-free soul; honoured with 8 gunas.
Head-preceptor of the order; honoured with 36 gunas.
Teacher of the scriptures to monks; honoured with 25 gunas.
An ascetic monk; honoured with 27 gunas. With sadhvis, shravakas and shravikas these form the four-fold Sangha.
Lay devotee, male/female; honoured with 21 gunas.
The four-fold Jain community (sadhu, sadhvi, shravaka, shravika), here 'appeased' when displeased.
Fortnightly (the pratikramana of the chaudas); the word replaced by 'chaumasi' or 'samvatsari' in the other observances.
The reigning head of the gaccha, invoked by the incumbent's name.
Ornament, adornment — the epithet given to each teacher as the glory of his gaccha.
The 'party of [correct] procedure' — the self-designation of the Anchalgachchha.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply follow the descending ladder of reverence — forgiveness asked of the Jinas, then the teachers, then the monks and lay-folk, then the whole Sangha and the lineage — and feel the song's single mood of humility. An advanced practitioner may dwell on the guna-counts (noting how the first five sum to the 108 Parameshthin qualities) and on the gaccha-specific naming of Aryarakshitasuri, Kalyanasagarasuri and the living gacchadhipati that locates this khamana within the Anchalgachchha succession.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse text, ritual placement within the Pakkhi pratikramana, and the pakkhi/chaumasi/samvatsari substitution rule

sources.title: Natubhai Shah, Jainism: The World of Conquerors
sources.consultedFor: General history of the Anchalgachchha Vidhipaksha gaccha and its acharyas Aryarakshitasuri and Kalyanasagarasuri

notes: The composition's author and date are not named in the source book and are left uncertain rather than invented. The text is vernacular classical Gujarati, not Prakrit; verse 6 (current gacchadhipati) and verse 7 (Chaumasi guru) are marked with asterisks in the book and are sung with the incumbent's name and on the relevant occasion. Verse 4's 'chaumasi tran var' (three times) reflects the thrice-yearly season-end Chaumasi. The guna-counts follow the standard Jain reckoning (Arihant 12, Siddha 8, Acharya 36, Upadhyaya 25, Sadhu 27 = 108; Shravaka/Shravika 21); the book's spellings and spaced bars are preserved verbatim and not normalized.
